name: Coast Guard Base Boston
tagline: The Coast Guard's First District headquarters and a cutter homeport on the Boston waterfront.
_note: Coast Guard Base Boston (Boston waterfront, MA). CONUS full template (BAH MHA MA120 Boston; RentCast/SchoolDigger work). CG uses same BAH tables. District 1 HQ + cutter homeport. Medical: CG clinic + TRICARE network (expensive Boston). Facts verified from official sources (uscg.mil, TRICARE, CGX, Wikipedia for corroborated headline facts). Massachusetts is TRICARE East. High-cost Boston metro. Medium-confidence flags: base coords (approx), off-base ZIP 02151 (Revere).

data.Also hosts: Cutters and Boston-area Coast Guard commands
data.Setting: A major historic East Coast city (high cost of living)

sections.title: Mission & Identity
data: Coast Guard Base Boston is the headquarters of the Coast Guard's First District, which oversees operations across the Northeast, and it homeports cutters on the Boston waterfront.
It's in the heart of historic Boston, a world-class city with a correspondingly high cost of living that shapes where families end up living.

sections.title: Big-City Boston Living: Commute vs. Price

data.subtitle: Boston is expensive and dense. The main choice is how far out you'll go (and which transit line) to make your BAH stretch.
intro: Housing is the challenge here. Coast Guard housing is very limited, so most families rent across the metro, trading commute for price.
Where you live mostly comes down to commute and schools across the city neighborhoods, the North Shore, and the suburbs, with the T (subway/commuter rail) shaping the decision.

recommendations.advice: North Shore communities (Revere, Chelsea, Winthrop) and the city keep you near the waterfront base.
Farther north or out along commuter rail trades a longer commute for lower prices.
Suburbs with strong schools sit farther out; weigh the commute and traffic.
Commuter rail and the subway make a longer-distance home livable; factor transit into where you live.

sections.title: Gates & Access
data: Visitors without a DoD/CG ID need to check in at the visitor control point.
Gate hours and visitor requirements change, so confirm the current details on the official Coast Guard site before you go.

sections.title: On-Base Housing
data: Coast Guard family housing in the Boston area is very limited, so most families rent across the metro.
Eligibility and availability vary, so start with the Coast Guard housing office early for current options and waitlists.

source.label: DoD 2026 BAH Rates, MHA MA120 (Boston) — Coast Guard uses the same BAH tables

sections.title: Schools
data: There is no DoDEA district here; children attend the local civilian public schools. Boston and the surrounding North Shore and suburban districts serve military families depending on where you live.
Your assigned schools depend on your address, so confirm the district and campus for any neighborhood you are considering.

data.Care: On-base clinic care for the assigned population; most family care is via the TRICARE network and Boston-area hospitals

data.Commissary: Nearest DoD commissary is at Hanscom AFB (Bedford, MA)

intro: Whether to rent or buy is the biggest money decision of most PCS moves, and in the pricey Boston metro buying is out of reach for most on a single tour.
rent: Your tour is a standard length and you'll PCS out.
Boston prices put a purchase out of comfortable reach for your budget.
You prefer renting for flexibility in an expensive market.
You want to keep your commute and budget manageable.
buy: You expect a longer tour or repeat New England assignments.
You can afford the high entry price and want to build equity with your VA loan.
You've found a suburb and commute you're confident about.
You're comfortable owning (and possibly renting out) in a high-cost market.
data.bottom_line: There is no universal right answer, but Boston's high prices make buying a big commitment, so most families rent. Run your BAH against the rent and home prices above, weigh your commute, and talk to a lender and an agent who work with military families before you commit.

intro: The first month at a new duty station is mostly logistics. In a big, expensive metro, starting early pays off.

steps.title: Line up temporary lodging
steps.text: Book lodging or a short-term rental before you arrive. You can claim Temporary Lodging Expense (TLE) for a limited number of days while you house-hunt.

steps.title: In-process and update DEERS
steps.text: Report to your unit and finance, and make sure DEERS and ID cards are current for every family member so TRICARE and base access work.

steps.title: Plan around commute
steps.text: CG housing is very limited; pick your area around commute and the T, and get the housing office's read before you sign anything.

steps.title: Register your vehicle and sort access
steps.text: Handle base access and Massachusetts vehicle registration, and learn the visitor process for guests. Bring your registration and insurance.

steps.title: Enroll the kids in school
steps.text: Contact the gaining district early and bring records and immunizations.

steps.title: Set up healthcare
steps.text: Update your TRICARE enrollment and pick a network provider; the base clinic serves the assigned population.

data.a: It's the Coast Guard's First District headquarters and a cutter homeport on the Boston waterfront.

data.q: Do Coast Guard families get BAH?
data.a: Yes, the same BAH tables as the rest of the military. Boston is MHA MA120.
Across the metro. The North Shore and city are closest; farther out along commuter rail is more affordable. CG housing is very limited.
Yes. Boston is a high-cost metro, so commute versus price (and the T) drive where you live.
